skill.name: meta-forest-model-plot
skill.description: Generate forest plots for meta-analysis of survival data. Input is a CSV file containing study names, HR and 95% confidence intervals, output forest plot PNG and data table CSV. Supports both R and Python scripts.
skill.summary: Automated forest plot generation for meta-analysis of survival data from CSV input
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 54 static findings were evaluated as false positives or benign operations. The 17 high-severity 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives triggered by documentation text and argparse strings. The 34 'shell backtick execution' findings are all markdown code block formatting in SKILL.md, not executable code. The 6 'system reconnaissance' findings are matplotlib plot configuration and data validation logic. The only genuine operations are legitimate: os.makedirs for output directory creation (forest_survival.py:211) and a GitHub source attribution URL (SKILL.md:7). Previous audit confirms security gate PASS, score 89/100, and Production Ready grade.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem write operations for output generation
medium_findings.description: The Python script creates output directories and writes PNG and CSV files to the filesystem using os.makedirs. This is expected behavior for a data visualization tool that produces plot images and data tables as its primary output.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded source attribution URL in documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded GitHub URL pointing to the skill source repository. This is a benign citation link for attribution purposes, not data exfiltration.

content.user_title: Generate Forest Plots for Meta-Analysis of Survival Data
content.value_statement: Medical researchers need to visualize meta-analysis results but creating publication-quality forest plots requires specialized statistical software. This skill automates forest plot generation from a simple CSV file containing hazard ratios and confidence intervals, producing ready-to-use PNG images and data tables.
actual_capabilities: Read CSV data with hazard ratios and confidence intervals for multiple studies
Compute pooled effect size using inverse-variance weighted meta-analysis
Calculate heterogeneity statistics including I-squared and Tau-squared
Generate publication-ready forest plot in PNG format at 300 DPI
Produce structured data table with per-study and pooled results as CSV
Support both Python and R execution paths for flexible deployment
limitations: Requires hazard ratios with 95% confidence intervals already extracted from source publications
Does not perform literature search or data extraction from research papers
Cannot interpret or provide clinical conclusions beyond computed statistics
Supports only fixed-effects and DerSimonian-Laird random-effects meta-analysis models

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Data Visualization
use_cases.description: A researcher conducting a systematic review of treatment effects can compile hazard ratios from published studies into a CSV file and generate a forest plot for their manuscript.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researcher or systematic review author

use_cases.title: Rapid Results Presentation
use_cases.description: A clinical analyst needs to quickly visualize survival outcomes across multiple clinical trials for a presentation or regulatory submission.

use_cases.title: Teaching Meta-Analysis Methods
use_cases.description: An epidemiology instructor demonstrates how pooled effect sizes and heterogeneity statistics are calculated from individual study results.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Forest Plot from CSV
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a forest plot from this CSV data: [upload CSV file with study, group1_HR, group1_95%Lower CI, group1_95%Upper CI columns]
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a prepared CSV file with study data and wants a quick visualization

prompt_templates.title: Custom Outcome Label Forest Plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a forest plot for Overall Survival outcome using the data in [file path]. Use the Python script and save output to [output directory].
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to specify the outcome indicator name and output location

prompt_templates.title: Multiple Outcome Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I have survival data for three outcomes (Overall Survival, Progression-Free Survival, and Disease-Free Survival) in separate CSV files. Generate a forest plot for each outcome and summarize the pooled hazard ratios in a comparison table.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs forest plots for multiple endpoints from the same set of studies

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Plot with Quality Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the CSV file at [path], validate that all required columns exist and contain positive numbers, then generate a forest plot with heterogeneity statistics. If any study has invalid data, report which studies are excluded and why.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants thorough data validation before visualization

output_examples.input: CSV file with 5 studies reporting hazard ratios for the Overall Survival outcome
output: Forest plot PNG image showing individual study HRs as red squares with confidence interval lines, pooled effect size as a black diamond, and a vertical dashed reference line at HR=1. Plot includes study labels on the y-axis and a log-scaled hazard ratio on the x-axis.
Data table CSV with columns for Study name, Hazard Ratio, 95% Confidence Interval, weight percentage, P value, logHR, and Standard Error. The final row contains the pooled model results.

output_examples.input: Validation request for a CSV with 3 studies for Progression-Free Survival endpoint
output: Validation summary confirming all required columns (study, group1_HR, confidence limits) are present and all values are positive numbers. Any invalid entries are reported with specific reasons for exclusion.
Forest plot and CSV data table generated with I-squared heterogeneity statistic, Tau-squared, and Q-test P value included in the output summary.

best_practices: Always validate input data before generating plots to catch missing columns or invalid values early
Use the R script path when available as it leverages established meta-analysis packages (meta, metafor) with robust statistical methods
Specify an explicit output directory to keep generated files organized, especially when processing multiple outcomes
anti_patterns: Do not fabricate or infer hazard ratios from incomplete data - the skill requires pre-extracted values with confidence intervals
Do not skip data validation - missing or invalid values produce misleading forest plots and incorrect pooled statistics
Do not use the skill for causal interpretation - forest plots show statistical association, not causation

faq.question: What input format does the skill require?
faq.answer: The skill requires a CSV file with columns for study name, hazard ratio (group1_HR), and 95% confidence interval bounds (group1_95Percent Lower CI or group1_95.Lower.CI, group1_95Percent Upper CI or group1_95.Upper.CI). An optional outcome_new column provides the outcome indicator name.

faq.question: Can I use this skill with non-survival data?
faq.answer: The skill is designed for hazard ratios from survival analysis. For odds ratios or risk ratios, you would need to modify the column mapping and axis labels in the scripts.

faq.question: Does the skill perform literature search or data extraction?
faq.answer: No. The skill only generates forest plots from data you already have. It does not search PubMed, extract data from PDFs, or perform literature review functions.

faq.question: Which execution path should I use, Python or R?
faq.answer: The R script is recommended when R and the required packages (meta, metafor, grid, stringr) are available. The Python script serves as a backup and requires only common Python libraries (pandas, numpy, matplotlib, scipy).

faq.question: What statistics does the forest plot include?
faq.answer: The output includes per-study and pooled hazard ratios with 95 percent confidence intervals, I-squared heterogeneity statistic, Tau-squared, Q-test P value, and study weights. Both fixed-effects and random-effects results using the DerSimonian-Laird method are computed.

faq.question: Can I customize the forest plot appearance?
faq.answer: Basic customization is available by modifying script parameters such as output directory and outcome label. For advanced visual customization like colors, fonts, or dimensions, you would need to edit the plotting code directly.
